    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 735
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/735](https://www.erdosproblems.com/735)
    24→
    25→Given any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, when can one assign positive weights to the points
    26→such that the sum of the weights along every line containing at least two points
    27→is the same?
    28→
    29→A problem of Murty, who conjectured this is only possible in one of four cases:
    30→1. All points on a line
    31→2. No three points on a line (general position)
    32→3. $n - 1$ points on a line (with one point off)
    33→4. The 7-point incenter configuration: a triangle, the feet of the three angle
    34→   bisectors, and the incenter (or a projective equivalence)
    35→
    36→Proved by Ackerman, Buchin, Knauer, Pinchasi, and Rote [ABKPR08].
    37→
    38→*Acknowledgement:* Noga Alon.
    39→
    40→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    41→  Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25–42.
    42→
    43→[ABKPR08] Ackerman, E., Buchin, K., Knauer, C., Pinchasi, R., and Rote, G.,
    44→_There are not too many magic configurations_, Discrete & Computational Geometry 39 (2008), 3--16.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open Finset BigOperators Classical EuclideanGeometry
    48→
    49→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    50→
    51→namespace Erdos735
    52→
    53→/-- A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ admits a balanced weighting if there exist positive
    54→real weights such that the total weight on every line through $\geq 2$ points is the same. -/
    55→noncomputable def AdmitsBalancedWeighting (S : Finset ℝ²) : Prop :=
    56→  ∃ (w : ℝ² → ℝ),
    57→    (∀ p ∈ S, w p > 0) ∧
    58→    ∀ p₁ ∈ S, ∀ q₁ ∈ S, ∀ p₂ ∈ S, ∀ q₂ ∈ S,
    59→      p₁ ≠ q₁ → p₂ ≠ q₂ →
    60→      ∑ x ∈ S.filter (fun x => Collinear ℝ ({p₁, q₁, x} : Set ℝ²)), w x =
    61→      ∑ x ∈ S.filter (fun x => Collinear ℝ ({p₂, q₂, x} : Set ℝ²)), w x
    62→
    63→/-- All points of $S$ are collinear (lie on a single line). -/
    64→def AllCollinear (S : Finset ℝ²) : Prop :=
    65→  Collinear ℝ (↑S : Set ℝ²)
    66→
    67→/-- All but one point of $S$ are collinear, and the full set is not collinear. -/
    68→def AllButOneCollinear (S : Finset ℝ²) : Prop :=
    69→  ∃ p ∈ S, Collinear ℝ (↑(S.erase p) : Set ℝ²) ∧ ¬Collinear ℝ (↑S : Set ℝ²)
    70→
    71→/-- The 7-point incenter configuration of a non-degenerate triangle $ABC$: the three vertices,
    72→the incenter, and the three feet of the angle bisectors. Two 7-point sets have the same
    73→incidence structure if there is a bijection between them that preserves collinearity of triples.
    74→
    75→`HasIncenterIncidenceStructure S` holds when $S$ has the same collinearity incidence pattern
    76→as some incenter configuration — equivalently, $S$ is a projective image of an incenter
    77→configuration where all 7 points remain finite. -/
    78→noncomputable def HasIncenterIncidenceStructure (S : Finset ℝ²) : Prop :=
    79→  ∃ (A B C : ℝ²),
    80→    ¬Collinear ℝ ({A, B, C} : Set ℝ²) ∧ (
    81→      let a := dist B C
    82→      let b := dist C A
    83→      let c := dist A B
    84→      let bisA := (b / (b + c)) • B + (c / (b + c)) • C
    85→      let bisB := (a / (a + c)) • A + (c / (a + c)) • C
    86→      let bisC := (a / (a + b)) • A + (b / (a + b)) • B
    87→      let inc := (a / (a + b + c)) • A + (b / (a + b + c)) • B + (c / (a + b + c)) • C
    88→      let T : Finset ℝ² := {A, B, C, bisA, bisB, bisC, inc}
    89→      ∃ f : ℝ² → ℝ²,
    90→        Function.Bijective f ∧
    91→        (S.image f = T ∨ S = T) ∧
    92→        ∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, ∀ r ∈ S,
    93→          Collinear ℝ ({p, q, r} : Set ℝ²) ↔
    94→          Collinear ℝ ({f p, f q, f r} : Set ℝ²))
    95→
    96→/--
    97→Erdős Problem 735 (Murty's conjecture, proved by Ackerman–Buchin–Knauer–Pinchasi–Rote [ABKPR08]):
    98→
    99→A finite set $S$ of at least 2 points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ admits a balanced weighting if and only if
   100→$S$ is one of:
   101→1. All points collinear,
   102→2. No three points collinear (general position),
   103→3. All but one point collinear, or
   104→4. A 7-point configuration with the same incidence structure as the incenter configuration
   105→   (i.e., the incenter configuration or a projective equivalence).
   106→-/
   107→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
   108→theorem erdos_735 (S : Finset ℝ²) (hS : 2 ≤ S.card) :
   109→    AdmitsBalancedWeighting S ↔
   110→      AllCollinear S ∨ NonTrilinear (↑S : Set ℝ²) ∨ AllButOneCollinear S ∨
   111→      HasIncenterIncidenceStructure S := by
   112→  sorry
   113→
   114→end Erdos735
   115→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 736
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/736](https://www.erdosproblems.com/736)
    24→
    25→A conjecture of Walter Taylor. Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$.
    26→Is there, for every cardinal number $m$, some graph $G_m$ of chromatic number $m$
    27→such that every finite subgraph of $G_m$ is a subgraph of $G$?
    28→
    29→Komjáth and Shelah [KoSh05] proved that it is consistent that the answer is no,
    30→so the conjecture is not provable in ZFC.
    31→
    32→[KoSh05] Komjáth, P. and Shelah, S., _Finite subgraphs of uncountably chromatic
    33→graphs_. J. Graph Theory 49 (2005), 28-38.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    37→
    38→universe u
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos736
    41→
    42→/-- $G$ contains a copy of $H$: there is an injective map preserving adjacency. -/
    43→def ContainsCopy {V W : Type*}
    44→    (G : SimpleGraph V) (H : SimpleGraph W) : Prop :=
    45→  ∃ f : W → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ u v, H.Adj u v → G.Adj (f u) (f v)
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős Problem 736 [Er81] [Er93, p. 343]:
    49→
    50→If $G$ is a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$, then for every cardinal $m$,
    51→there exists a graph $G_m$ with chromatic number $m$ such that every finite
    52→subgraph of $G_m$ is also a subgraph of $G$.
    53→
    54→A conjecture of Walter Taylor. Komjáth and Shelah [KoSh05] proved that
    55→this is consistently false (not provable in ZFC).
    56→-/
    57→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    58→theorem erdos_736 : answer(sorry) ↔
    59→    ∀ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    60→    G.chromaticCardinal = aleph 1 →
    61→    ∀ (m : Cardinal.{u}),
    62→    ∃ (W : Type u) (Gm : SimpleGraph W),
    63→      Gm.chromaticCardinal = m ∧
    64→      ∀ (U : Type u) [Fintype U] (H : SimpleGraph U),
    65→        ContainsCopy Gm H → ContainsCopy G H := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos736
    69→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 737
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/737](https://www.erdosproblems.com/737)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Must there exist an
    25→edge $e$ such that, for all large $n$, $G$ contains a cycle of length $n$
    26→containing $e$?
    27→
    28→A problem of Erdős, Hajnal, and Shelah [EHS74], who proved that $G$ must
    29→contain all sufficiently large cycles (see [594]).
    30→
    31→This is true, and was proved by Thomassen [Th83].
    32→
    33→## References
    34→
    35→[EHS74] Erdős, P., Hajnal, A., and Shelah, S., *On some general properties of chromatic numbers*.
    36→Topics in topology (Proc. Colloq., Keszthely, 1972), Colloq. Math. Soc. Janos Bolyai (1974),
    37→243-255.
    38→
    39→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    40→Combinatorica **1** (1981), 25–42.
    41→
    42→[Th83] Thomassen, C., _Cycles in graphs of uncountable chromatic number_,
    43→Combinatorica **3** (1983), 133–134.
    44→-/
    45→
    46→open SimpleGraph
    47→
    48→namespace Erdos737
    49→
    50→/-- A graph has uncountable chromatic number: it cannot be properly colored
    51→    with countably many colors. -/
    52→def HasUncountableChromaticNumber {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
    53→  ∀ (α : Type*) [Countable α], IsEmpty (G.Coloring α)
    54→
    55→/-- The cycle graph $C_m$ on $m$ vertices ($m \geq 3$). Vertex $i$ is adjacent to vertex
    56→    $i + 1 \pmod{m}$ and vertex $i - 1 \pmod{m}$. -/
    57→def cycleGraph (m : ℕ) (_ : m ≥ 3) : SimpleGraph (Fin m) where
    58→  Adj i j := i ≠ j ∧ (j.val = (i.val + 1) % m ∨ i.val = (j.val + 1) % m)
    59→  symm := fun _ _ ⟨hne, h⟩ => ⟨hne.symm, h.elim Or.inr Or.inl⟩
    60→  loopless := fun _ ⟨h, _⟩ => h rfl
    61→
    62→/-- $G$ contains a copy of the cycle graph $C_m$ passing through edge $\{u, v\}$:
    63→    there is an injective embedding of $C_m$ into $G$ that maps some edge of
    64→    the cycle to the edge $\{u, v\}$. -/
    65→def ContainsCycleThroughEdge {V : Type*}
    66→    (G : SimpleGraph V) (u v : V) (m : ℕ) (hm : m ≥ 3) : Prop :=
    67→  ∃ f : Fin m → V, Function.Injective f ∧
    68→    (∀ a b : Fin m, (cycleGraph m hm).Adj a b → G.Adj (f a) (f b)) ∧
    69→    ∃ a b : Fin m, (cycleGraph m hm).Adj a b ∧
    70→      ((f a = u ∧ f b = v) ∨ (f a = v ∧ f b = u))
    71→
    72→/--
    73→Erdős Problem 737 [EHS74] [Er81]:
    74→
    75→If $G$ is a graph with chromatic number $\geq \aleph_1$ (uncountable), then there
    76→exists an edge $\{u, v\}$ of $G$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
    77→$G$ contains a cycle of length $n$ passing through $\{u, v\}$.
    78→
    79→Proved by Thomassen [Th83].
    80→-/
    81→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    82→theorem erdos_737 : answer(True) ↔
    83→    ∀ {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V),
    84→      HasUncountableChromaticNumber G →
    85→        ∃ u v : V, G.Adj u v ∧
    86→          ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ (n : ℕ) (hn : n ≥ 3), n ≥ N₀ →
    87→            ContainsCycleThroughEdge G u v n hn := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→end Erdos737
    91→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 738
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/738](https://www.erdosproblems.com/738)
    24→
    25→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_.
    26→Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25-42.
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open SimpleGraph
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos738
    32→
    33→/--
    34→Erdős Problem 738 (Gyárfás conjecture) [Er81]:
    35→
    36→If $G$ is a graph with infinite chromatic number and $G$ is triangle-free
    37→(contains no $K_3$), must $G$ contain every finite tree as an induced subgraph?
    38→-/
    39→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    40→theorem erdos_738 : answer(sorry) ↔
    41→    ∀ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    42→    χ(G) = ⊤ → G.CliqueFree 3 →
    43→    ∀ (m : ℕ) (T : SimpleGraph (Fin m)), T.IsTree →
    44→      Nonempty (T ↪g G) := by
    45→  sorry
    46→
    47→end Erdos738
    48→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 739
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/739](https://www.erdosproblems.com/739)
    24→
    25→If a graph has infinite chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$, must every infinite cardinal
    26→$\mathfrak{n} < \mathfrak{m}$ be realized as the chromatic number of some subgraph?
    27→
    28→A question of Galvin [Ga73], who proved the case $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$
    29→(i.e., every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_0$ has subgraphs of every finite
    30→chromatic number; note that under the formalization's restriction to infinite
    31→$\mathfrak{n}$, the case $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$ is vacuously true).
    32→Komjáth [Ko88b] proved it is consistent that the answer is no
    33→(with $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_2$ and $\mathfrak{n} = \aleph_1$,
    34→under $2^{\aleph_0} = 2^{\aleph_1} = 2^{\aleph_2} = \aleph_3$).
    35→Shelah [Sh90] proved that assuming $V = L$, the answer is yes
    36→with $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_2$ and $\mathfrak{n} = \aleph_1$.
    37→Whether the answer is affirmative under GCH remains open.
    38→
    39→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    40→  Combinatorica **1** (1981), 25–42.
    41→
    42→[Ga73] Galvin, F., _Chromatic numbers of subgraphs_. Period. Math. Hungar. (1973), 117–119.
    43→
    44→[Ko88b] Komjáth, P., _Consistency results on infinite graphs_. Israel J. Math. (1988), 285–294.
    45→
    46→[Sh90] Shelah, S., _Incompactness for chromatic numbers of graphs_. (1990), 361–371.
    47→-/
    48→
    49→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    50→
    51→universe u
    52→
    53→namespace Erdos739
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Erdős Problem 739 [Er81]:
    57→
    58→If $G$ is a graph with infinite chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$, then for every infinite
    59→cardinal $\mathfrak{n} < \mathfrak{m}$, there is a subgraph of $G$ with chromatic
    60→number $\mathfrak{n}$.
    61→
    62→A question of Galvin [Ga73]. This is not provable
    63→in ZFC: Komjáth [Ko88b] showed it is consistent that the answer is no.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    66→theorem erdos_739 : answer(sorry) ↔
    67→    ∀ {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V)
    68→    (𝔪 : Cardinal.{u}), ℵ₀ ≤ 𝔪 → G.chromaticCardinal = 𝔪 →
    69→    ∀ (𝔫 : Cardinal.{u}), ℵ₀ ≤ 𝔫 → 𝔫 < 𝔪 →
    70→    ∃ (W : Type u) (H : SimpleGraph W),
    71→      H.chromaticCardinal = 𝔫 ∧
    72→      ∃ f : W → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ a b, H.Adj a b → G.Adj (f a) (f b) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos739
    76→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 740
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/740](https://www.erdosproblems.com/740)
    24→
    25→A question of Erdős and Hajnal [Er69b][Er71, p.100][Er81][Er95d].
    26→
    27→Let $\mathfrak{m}$ be an infinite cardinal and $G$ be a graph with chromatic number
    28→$\mathfrak{m}$. Let $r \geq 1$. Must $G$ contain a subgraph of chromatic number
    29→$\mathfrak{m}$ which does not contain any odd cycle of length $\leq r$?
    30→
    31→Rödl proved this is true if $\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$ and $r = 3$ (see [108] for
    32→the finitary version).
    33→
    34→More generally, Erdős and Hajnal asked whether for every cardinal $\mathfrak{m}$ and
    35→integer $r$, there exists $f_r(\mathfrak{m})$ such that every graph with chromatic
    36→number $\geq f_r(\mathfrak{m})$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number
    37→$\mathfrak{m}$ with no odd cycle of length $\leq r$.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    41→
    42→universe u
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos740
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 740 [Er69b][Er71, p.100][Er81][Er95d]:
    48→
    49→If $G$ is a graph with infinite chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$, then for every
    50→$r \geq 1$, $G$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number $\mathfrak{m}$ that has
    51→no odd cycle of length $\leq r$.
    52→
    53→A question of Erdős and Hajnal. Rödl proved the case
    54→$\mathfrak{m} = \aleph_0$, $r = 3$.
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    57→theorem erdos_740 : answer(sorry) ↔
    58→    ∀ {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) (𝔪 : Cardinal.{u}),
    59→      ℵ₀ ≤ 𝔪 → chromaticCardinal G = 𝔪 → ∀ (r : ℕ), 1 ≤ r →
    60→        ∃ (W : Type u) (H : SimpleGraph W),
    61→          chromaticCardinal H = 𝔪 ∧
    62→          (∃ f : W → V, Function.Injective f ∧ ∀ a b, H.Adj a b → G.Adj (f a) (f b)) ∧
    63→          (∀ (w : W) (p : H.Walk w w), p.IsCycle → Odd p.length → r < p.length) := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→end Erdos740
    67→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 742
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/742](https://www.erdosproblems.com/742)
    23→
    24→A conjecture of Murty and Plesnik (see [CaHa79]), also attributed to Ore.
    25→The complete bipartite graph shows that this would be best possible.
    26→This was proved for sufficiently large $n$ by Füredi [Fu92]; the conjecture
    27→as originally posed is for all $n$.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Classical SimpleGraph
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos742
    33→
    34→/--
    35→**Erdős Problem 742** [Er81]:
    36→
    37→Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with diameter $2$, such that deleting any edge
    38→increases the diameter (either by disconnecting the graph or increasing it
    39→beyond $2$). Then $G$ has at most $n^2/4$ edges.
    40→-/
    41→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    42→theorem erdos_742 : answer(True) ↔
    43→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)),
    44→      G.Connected →
    45→      G.diam = 2 →
    46→      (∀ v w : Fin n, G.Adj v w →
    47→        ¬(G.deleteEdges {Sym2.mk (v, w)}).Connected ∨
    48→        2 < (G.deleteEdges {Sym2.mk (v, w)}).diam) →
    49→      G.edgeFinset.card ≤ n ^ 2 / 4 := by
    50→  sorry
    51→
    52→end Erdos742
    53→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 743
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/743](https://www.erdosproblems.com/743)
    23→
    24→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be a collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    25→Can we always write $K_n$ as the edge disjoint union of the $T_k$?
    26→
    27→A conjecture of Gyárfás, known as the tree packing conjecture.
    28→
    29→Gyárfás and Lehel proved that this holds if all but at most 2 of the trees are
    30→stars, or if all the trees are stars or paths. Fishburn proved this for $n \leq 9$.
    31→Bollobás proved that the smallest $\lfloor n/\sqrt{2} \rfloor$ many trees can always be packed
    32→greedily into $K_n$.
    33→
    34→Joos, Kim, Kühn, and Osthus proved this conjecture holds when the trees
    35→have bounded maximum degree. Allen, Böttcher, Clemens, Hladký, Piguet, and Taraz
    36→proved it holds when all trees have maximum degree at most $c \cdot n / \log n$ for
    37→some constant $c > 0$. Janzer and Montgomery proved that there exists
    38→some $c > 0$ such that the largest $cn$ trees can be packed into $K_n$.
    39→
    40→[Er81] Erdős, P.
    41→[ABCHPT21] Allen, P., Böttcher, J., Clemens, D., Hladký, J., Piguet, D., Taraz, A.,
    42→  _The tree packing conjecture for trees of almost linear maximum degree_. arXiv:2106.11720 (2021).
    43→-/
    44→
    45→open SimpleGraph
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos743
    48→
    49→/--
    50→**Erdős Problem 743** [Er81]:
    51→
    52→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be any collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    53→Then the complete graph $K_n$ can be decomposed into edge-disjoint copies
    54→of these trees. Also known as the tree packing conjecture (Gyárfás).
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    57→theorem erdos_743 : answer(sorry) ↔
    58→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 2 ≤ n →
    59→    ∀ (T : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → SimpleGraph (Fin (k.val + 2))),
    60→    (∀ k, (T k).IsTree) →
    61→    ∃ (f : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → Fin (k.val + 2) → Fin n),
    62→      -- Each embedding is injective
    63→      (∀ k, Function.Injective (f k)) ∧
    64→      -- The edge images are pairwise disjoint across different trees
    65→      (∀ k₁ k₂, k₁ ≠ k₂ →
    66→        ∀ a₁ b₁, (T k₁).Adj a₁ b₁ →
    67→        ∀ a₂ b₂, (T k₂).Adj a₂ b₂ →
    68→        Sym2.mk (f k₁ a₁, f k₁ b₁) ≠ Sym2.mk (f k₂ a₂, f k₂ b₂)) ∧
    69→      -- Every edge of Kₙ is covered
    70→      (∀ v w : Fin n, v ≠ w →
    71→        ∃ k a b, (T k).Adj a b ∧
    72→          Sym2.mk (v, w) = Sym2.mk (f k a, f k b)) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos743
    76→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 744
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/744](https://www.erdosproblems.com/744)
    23→
    24→For a $k$-critical graph $G$ on $n$ vertices, let $f_k(n)$ denote the minimum number of edges
    25→that must be deleted from $G$ to make it bipartite. Erdős asked whether $f_k(n) \to \infty$ as
    26→$n \to \infty$ for $k \ge 4$. Rödl and Tuza disproved this by showing that $f_k(n) = \binom{k-1}{2}$
    27→for all sufficiently large $n$.
    28→
    29→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    30→  Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25–42.
    31→
    32→[EHS82] Erdős, P. and Hajnal, A. and Szemerédi, E.,
    33→  _On almost bipartite large chromatic graphs_, Theory and practice of combinatorics (1982), 117–123.
    34→
    35→[Ga68] Gallai, T., _On covering of graphs_, Theory of Graphs, Proc. Coll. Tihany, Hungary (1968),
    36→  231–236.
    37→
    38→[RoTu85] Rödl, V. and Tuza, Zs., _On color critical graphs_, J. Combin. Theory Ser. B (1985),
    39→  204–213.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Filter
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos744
    45→
    46→/-- A graph $G$ is $k$-critical if it has chromatic number exactly $k$ (i.e., is
    47→$k$-colorable but not $(k-1)$-colorable) and every proper subgraph (strictly
    48→fewer edges) is $(k-1)$-colorable. -/
    49→def IsKCritical {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    50→  G.Colorable k ∧ ¬G.Colorable (k - 1) ∧
    51→  ∀ H : SimpleGraph V, H < G → H.Colorable (k - 1)
    52→
    53→/-- The minimum number of edges to delete from $G$ to make it bipartite
    54→($2$-colorable). Defined as the infimum over cardinalities of edge sets
    55→whose removal yields a $2$-colorable graph. -/
    56→noncomputable def minEdgesToBipartite {V : Type*} [DecidableEq V]
    57→    (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ :=
    58→  sInf {m : ℕ | ∃ S : Finset (Sym2 V), S.card = m ∧
    59→    (G.deleteEdges (↑S : Set (Sym2 V))).Colorable 2}
    60→
    61→/-- $f_k(n)$: the minimum, over all $k$-critical graphs $G$ on $n$ vertices, of the
    62→minimum number of edges to delete from $G$ to make it bipartite. -/
    63→noncomputable def fk (k n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    64→  sInf {m : ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n),
    65→    IsKCritical G k ∧ minEdgesToBipartite G = m}
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős Problem 744 (Disproved) [Er81, EHS82]:
    69→
    70→The original conjecture asked: is it true that $f_k(n) \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$ for $k \ge 4$?
    71→In particular, is it true that $f_4(n) \gg \log n$?
    72→
    73→Rödl and Tuza [RoTu85] disproved this by showing that $f_k(n) = \binom{k-1}{2}$
    74→for all sufficiently large $n$.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    77→theorem erdos_744 : answer(False) ↔
    78→    ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ 4 → Tendsto (fk k) atTop atTop := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos744
    82→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 745
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/745](https://www.erdosproblems.com/745)
    23→
    24→Describe the size of the second largest component of the random graph on $n$
    25→vertices, where each edge is included independently with probability $1/n$.
    26→
    27→Erdős believed that almost surely the second largest component has size
    28→$\ll \log n$. This is true, as proved by Komlós, Sulyok, and Szemerédi.
    29→
    30→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to
    31→see solved_. Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25-42.
    32→
    33→[KSS80] Komlós, J., Sulyok, M., and Szemerédi, E., _Linear problems in
    34→combinatorial number theory_. Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. 36 (1980), 341-365.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open SimpleGraph Finset
    38→
    39→attribute [local instance] Classical.propDecidable
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos745
    42→
    43→/-- An edge configuration on `Fin n`: a Boolean assignment to each
    44→unordered pair $\{i, j\}$ with $i < j$. -/
    45→abbrev EdgeConfig745 (n : ℕ) := {p : Fin n × Fin n // p.1 < p.2} → Bool
    46→
    47→/-- The simple graph on `Fin n` determined by an edge configuration. -/
    48→def toGraph745 {n : ℕ} (ec : EdgeConfig745 n) : SimpleGraph (Fin n) where
    49→  Adj u v := ∃ h : min u v < max u v, ec ⟨(min u v, max u v), h⟩ = true
    50→  symm := by
    51→    intro u v ⟨h, he⟩
    52→    refine ⟨by rwa [min_comm, max_comm] at h, ?_⟩
    53→    have : (⟨(min u v, max u v), h⟩ : {p : Fin n × Fin n // p.1 < p.2}) =
    54→           ⟨(min v u, max v u), by rwa [min_comm, max_comm] at h⟩ := by
    55→      ext <;> simp [min_comm, max_comm]
    56→    rwa [← this]
    57→  loopless := fun v ⟨h, _⟩ => by simp at h
    58→
    59→/-- The number of edges in an edge configuration on `Fin n`. -/
    60→def edgeCount745 {n : ℕ} (ec : EdgeConfig745 n) : ℕ :=
    61→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun p : {p : Fin n × Fin n // p.1 < p.2} => ec p = true)).card
    62→
    63→/-- The probability weight of a specific edge configuration under the
    64→Erdős–Rényi model $G(n, 1/n)$: each edge is included independently
    65→with probability $1/n$. -/
    66→noncomputable def gnpWeight745 (n : ℕ) (ec : EdgeConfig745 n) : ℝ :=
    67→  (1 / (n : ℝ)) ^ edgeCount745 ec *
    68→  (1 - 1 / (n : ℝ)) ^ (n.choose 2 - edgeCount745 ec)
    69→
    70→/-- The $G(n, 1/n)$-probability of a graph property $P$: the sum of weights
    71→over all edge configurations whose graph satisfies $P$. -/
    72→noncomputable def gnpProb745 (n : ℕ) (P : SimpleGraph (Fin n) → Prop) : ℝ :=
    73→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun ec : EdgeConfig745 n =>
    74→    P (toGraph745 ec))).sum (gnpWeight745 n)
    75→
    76→/-- The size of the connected component containing vertex $v$ in graph $G$. -/
    77→noncomputable def componentSize745 {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n))
    78→    (v : Fin n) : ℕ :=
    79→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun w : Fin n => G.Reachable v w)).card
    80→
    81→/-- The size of the second largest connected component of $G$ on `Fin n`.
    82→Defined as the smallest $k$ such that at most one connected component
    83→has more than $k$ vertices; equivalently, any two vertices whose
    84→components both exceed size $k$ must be in the same component. -/
    85→noncomputable def secondLargestComponent745 {n : ℕ}
    86→    (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    87→  sInf {k : ℕ | ∀ u v : Fin n,
    88→    componentSize745 G u > k → componentSize745 G v > k → G.Reachable u v}
    89→
    90→/--
    91→Erdős Problem 745 [Er81]:
    92→
    93→In the Erdős–Rényi random graph $G(n, 1/n)$, the second largest connected
    94→component almost surely has size $O(\log n)$.
    95→
    96→Formally: there exists a constant $C > 0$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$
    97→there exists $n_0$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$, the $G(n, 1/n)$-probability
    98→that the second largest component has size at most $C \cdot \log n$ is at least
    99→$1 - \varepsilon$.
   100→
   101→Proved by Komlós, Sulyok, and Szemerédi [KSS80].
   102→-/
   103→@[category research solved, AMS 5 60]
   104→theorem erdos_745 :
   105→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
   106→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
   107→    ∃ n₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ n₀ →
   108→      gnpProb745 n (fun G =>
   109→        (secondLargestComponent745 G : ℝ) ≤ C * Real.log (n : ℝ)) ≥ 1 - ε := by
   110→  sorry
   111→
   112→end Erdos745
   113→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 746
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/746](https://www.erdosproblems.com/746)
    23→
    24→A conjecture of Erdős and Rényi [ErRe66]. Pósa [Po76] showed this with $Cn \log n$ edges
    25→for some large $C$, Korshunov [Ko77] improved the threshold, and Komlós and Szemerédi [KoSz83]
    26→proved the sharp result that with $(1/2)n \log n + (1/2)n \log \log n + cn$ edges the probability
    27→of being Hamiltonian tends to $e^{-e^{-2c}}$.
    28→
    29→[Er71] Erdős, P., _Topics in combinatorial analysis_. Proc. Second Louisiana Conf. on
    30→Combinatorics, Graph Theory and Computing (1971), 2–20.
    31→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_.
    32→Combinatorica **1** (1981), 25–42.
    33→[Er82e] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on finite and infinite combinatorial analysis II_.
    34→L'Enseignement Math. **27** (1982), 163–176.
    35→[ErRe66] Erdős, P. and Rényi, A., _On the existence of a factor of degree one of a connected
    36→random graph_. Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. (1966), 359–368.
    37→[Po76] Pósa, L., _Hamiltonian circuits in random graphs_. Discrete Math. (1976), 359–364.
    38→[Ko77] Korshunov, A. D., _Solution of a problem of P. Erdős and A. Rényi on Hamiltonian cycles
    39→in nonoriented graphs_. Diskret. Analiz (1977), 17–56, 90.
    40→[KoSz83] Komlós, J. and Szemerédi, E., _Limit distribution for the existence of Hamiltonian
    41→cycles in a random graph_. Discrete Math. (1983), 55–63.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open SimpleGraph Finset
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos746
    47→
    48→/-- The simple graph on $\operatorname{Fin} n$ determined by a Boolean edge configuration. -/
    49→def toGraph {n : ℕ} (ec : Fin n → Fin n → Bool) : SimpleGraph (Fin n) where
    50→  Adj u v := u ≠ v ∧ ec (min u v) (max u v) = true
    51→  symm := fun _ _ ⟨hne, h⟩ => ⟨hne.symm, by rwa [min_comm, max_comm]⟩
    52→  loopless := fun v ⟨h, _⟩ => h rfl
    53→
    54→/-- The number of edges in a Boolean edge configuration on $\operatorname{Fin} n$
    55→(counting only pairs $i < j$ to avoid double-counting). -/
    56→def edgeCount {n : ℕ} (ec : Fin n → Fin n → Bool) : ℕ :=
    57→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun p : Fin n × Fin n => p.1 < p.2 ∧ ec p.1 p.2 = true)).card
    58→
    59→/-- The set of all edge configurations on $\operatorname{Fin} n$ with exactly $m$ edges. -/
    60→def graphsWithEdges (n m : ℕ) : Finset (Fin n → Fin n → Bool) :=
    61→  Finset.univ.filter (fun ec => edgeCount ec = m)
    62→
    63→open Classical in
    64→/-- The fraction of graphs on $\operatorname{Fin} n$ with exactly $m$ edges that satisfy
    65→property $P$ (the $G(n,m)$ probability of $P$). Returns $0$ if there are
    66→no graphs with exactly $m$ edges. -/
    67→noncomputable def gnmFraction (n m : ℕ) (P : SimpleGraph (Fin n) → Prop) : ℝ :=
    68→  ((graphsWithEdges n m).filter (fun ec => P (toGraph ec))).card /
    69→  ((graphsWithEdges n m).card : ℝ)
    70→
    71→/--
    72→Erdős Problem 746 [Er71, p.98], [Er81, p.16], [Er82e, p.69]:
    73→
    74→For every $\varepsilon > 0$, almost surely a random graph on $n$ vertices with at least
    75→$(1/2 + \varepsilon) \cdot n \cdot \log n$ edges is Hamiltonian.
    76→
    77→Formally: for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and $\delta > 0$, there exists $n_0$ such that for all
    78→$n \geq n_0$ and all $m$ with $(1/2 + \varepsilon) \cdot n \cdot \log n \leq m \leq \binom{n}{2}$,
    79→the $G(n,m)$-probability that the graph is Hamiltonian is at least $1 - \delta$.
    80→
    81→Proved by Komlós and Szemerédi [KoSz83].
    82→-/
    83→@[category research solved, AMS 5 60]
    84→theorem erdos_746 : answer(True) ↔
    85→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    86→    ∀ δ : ℝ, δ > 0 →
    87→    ∃ n₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ n₀ →
    88→      ∀ m : ℕ, (m : ℝ) ≥ (1/2 + ε) * (n : ℝ) * Real.log (n : ℝ) →
    89→        m ≤ n.choose 2 →
    90→        gnmFraction n m (fun G => G.IsHamiltonian) ≥ 1 - δ := by
    91→  sorry
    92→
    93→end Erdos746
    94→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 747
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/747](https://www.erdosproblems.com/747)
    23→
    24→Shamir's problem (1979): How large should $\ell(n)$ be such that, almost surely, a random
    25→$3$-uniform hypergraph on $3n$ vertices with $\ell(n)$ edges must contain $n$ vertex-disjoint
    26→edges?
    27→
    28→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    29→  Combinatorica **1** (1981), 25–42.
    30→
    31→[JKV08] Johansson, A., Kahn, J., and Vu, V., _Factors in random graphs_, Random Structures
    32→& Algorithms 33 (2008), 1-28.
    33→
    34→[Ka23] Kahn, J., _Asymptotics for Shamir's problem_, Annals of Mathematics 198 (2023), 1-69.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open scoped Classical
    38→open Finset
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos747
    41→
    42→/-- The set of all $3$-element subsets of $\operatorname{Fin} N$ (potential hyperedges). -/
    43→def threeEdges (N : ℕ) : Finset (Finset (Fin N)) :=
    44→  Finset.univ.powersetCard 3
    45→
    46→/-- The set of all $3$-uniform hypergraphs on $\operatorname{Fin} N$ with exactly $m$ edges. -/
    47→def hypergraphsWithEdges (N m : ℕ) : Finset (Finset (Finset (Fin N))) :=
    48→  (threeEdges N).powersetCard m
    49→
    50→/-- A $3$-uniform hypergraph $H$ contains a matching of size $k$: there exist
    51→    $k$ pairwise vertex-disjoint edges in $H$. -/
    52→def HasMatching {N : ℕ} (H : Finset (Finset (Fin N))) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    53→  ∃ M : Finset (Finset (Fin N)), M ⊆ H ∧ M.card = k ∧
    54→    ∀ e₁ ∈ M, ∀ e₂ ∈ M, e₁ ≠ e₂ → Disjoint e₁ e₂
    55→
    56→/-- The fraction of $3$-uniform hypergraphs on $\operatorname{Fin} N$ with $m$ edges that
    57→    contain a matching of size $k$. Returns $0$ if no such hypergraphs exist. -/
    58→noncomputable def hypergraphMatchingFraction (N m k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    59→  ((hypergraphsWithEdges N m).filter (fun H => HasMatching H k)).card /
    60→  ((hypergraphsWithEdges N m).card : ℝ)
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 747 (Shamir's problem):
    64→
    65→For every $\varepsilon > 0$, almost surely a random $3$-uniform hypergraph on $3n$ vertices
    66→with at least $(1 + \varepsilon) \cdot n \cdot \log n$ edges contains $n$ vertex-disjoint edges.
    67→
    68→Formally: for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and $\delta > 0$, there exists $n_0$ such that for all
    69→$n \geq n_0$ and all $m$ with $(1 + \varepsilon) \cdot n \cdot \log n \leq m \leq \binom{3n}{3}$,
    70→the fraction of $3$-uniform hypergraphs on $3n$ vertices with $m$ edges containing a perfect
    71→matching is at least $1 - \delta$.
    72→
    73→Proved by Johansson, Kahn, and Vu [JKV08]; sharp threshold by Kahn [Ka23].
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    76→theorem erdos_747 :
    77→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    78→    ∀ δ : ℝ, δ > 0 →
    79→    ∃ n₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ n₀ →
    80→      ∀ m : ℕ, (m : ℝ) ≥ (1 + ε) * (n : ℝ) * Real.log (n : ℝ) →
    81→        m ≤ (3 * n).choose 3 →
    82→        hypergraphMatchingFraction (3 * n) m n ≥ 1 - δ := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos747
    86→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 748
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/748](https://www.erdosproblems.com/748)
    23→
    24→The Cameron-Erdős Conjecture. Let $f(n)$ count the number of sum-free subsets
    25→$A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$, i.e., $A$ contains no three elements $a$, $b$, $c$ with
    26→$a = b + c$. Is it true that $f(n) = 2^{(1+o(1)) n/2}$?
    27→
    28→The lower bound $f(n) \geq 2^{n/2}$ is trivial (consider all subsets of
    29→$\{\lceil n/2 \rceil, \ldots, n\}$).
    30→
    31→This was proved independently by Green [Gr04] and Sapozhenko [Sa03], who showed
    32→$f(n) \sim c_n \cdot 2^{n/2}$ where $c_n$ depends on the parity of $n$.
    33→
    34→See also Problem 877 (maximal sum-free subsets) and OEIS sequence A007865.
    35→
    36→[CaEr90] Cameron, P.J. and Erdős, P.
    37→
    38→[Er94b] Erdős, P.
    39→
    40→[Er98] Erdős, P.
    41→
    42→[Gr04] Green, B., _The Cameron-Erdős conjecture_, Bull. London Math. Soc. 36 (2004), 769-778.
    43→
    44→[Sa03] Sapozhenko, A.A., _The Cameron-Erdős conjecture_, Discrete Math. 235 (2003), 131-138.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open Finset
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos748
    50→
    51→/-- A finite set of natural numbers is *sum-free* if it contains no three
    52→    elements $a$, $b$, $c$ (not necessarily distinct) with $b + c = a$. Equivalently,
    53→    for all $b, c \in A$, we have $b + c \notin A$. -/
    54→def IsSumFree (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    55→  ∀ b ∈ A, ∀ c ∈ A, b + c ∉ A
    56→
    57→instance : DecidablePred IsSumFree :=
    58→  fun A => decidable_of_iff (∀ b ∈ A, ∀ c ∈ A, b + c ∉ A) Iff.rfl
    59→
    60→/-- The number of sum-free subsets of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$. -/
    61→noncomputable def sumFreeSubsetCount (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    62→  ((Finset.Icc 1 n).powerset.filter (fun A => IsSumFree A)).card
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Erdős Problem 748 (Cameron-Erdős Conjecture) [CaEr90][Er94b][Er98]:
    66→
    67→Let $f(n)$ count the number of sum-free subsets of $\{1, \ldots, n\}$. Then
    68→$$f(n) = 2^{(1+o(1)) n/2}.$$
    69→
    70→Equivalently: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    71→$$2^{(1 - \varepsilon) \cdot n/2} \leq f(n) \leq 2^{(1 + \varepsilon) \cdot n/2}.$$
    72→
    73→Proved independently by Green [Gr04] and Sapozhenko [Sa03].
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    76→theorem erdos_748 : answer(True) ↔
    77→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    78→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    79→      (2 : ℝ) ^ ((1 - ε) * (n : ℝ) / 2) ≤ (sumFreeSubsetCount n : ℝ) ∧
    80→      (sumFreeSubsetCount n : ℝ) ≤ (2 : ℝ) ^ ((1 + ε) * (n : ℝ) / 2) := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos748
    84→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 750
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/750](https://www.erdosproblems.com/750)
    23→
    24→Does there exist, for every function $f$ with $f(m) \to \infty$, a graph with infinite chromatic
    25→number such that every finite set of $m$ vertices contains an independent set of size at least
    26→$m/2 - f(m)$?
    27→
    28→See also [Problem #75](https://www.erdosproblems.com/75).
    29→
    30→[Er69b] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in chromatic graph theory_. Proof Techniques in Graph
    31→Theory (1969), 27-35.
    32→
    33→[ErHa67b] Erdős, P. and Hajnal, A., _On chromatic graphs_. Mat. Lapok (1967), 1-4.
    34→
    35→[EHS82] Erdős, P., Hajnal, A., and Szemerédi, E., _On almost bipartite large chromatic
    36→graphs_. Theory and practice of combinatorics (1982), 117-123.
    37→
    38→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_ (1994).
    39→
    40→[Er95d] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in discrete mathematics_ (1995).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open SimpleGraph
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos750
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős Problem 750 [Er94b][Er95d]:
    49→
    50→Does there exist, for every function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with $f(m) \to \infty$
    51→as $m \to \infty$, a graph $G$ with infinite chromatic number such that every finite set of
    52→$m$ vertices in $G$ contains an independent set of size at least $m/2 - f(m)$?
    53→
    54→In [Er69b] Erdős conjectures this for $f(m) = \varepsilon m$ for any fixed $\varepsilon > 0$.
    55→This follows from a result of Erdős, Hajnal, and Szemerédi [EHS82].
    56→
    57→In [ErHa67b] Erdős and Hajnal prove this for $f(m) \geq cm$ for all $c > 1/4$.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    60→theorem erdos_750 : answer(sorry) ↔
    61→    ∀ f : ℕ → ℕ, (∀ C : ℕ, ∃ M₀ : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, m ≥ M₀ → f m ≥ C) →
    62→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    63→      (∀ k : ℕ, ¬G.Colorable k) ∧
    64→      ∀ (S : Finset V), ∃ I : Finset V,
    65→        I ⊆ S ∧ G.IsIndepSet ↑I ∧
    66→        (S.card : ℝ) / 2 - (f S.card : ℝ) ≤ (I.card : ℝ) := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/--
    70→Erdős Problem 750 — εm variant [EHS82]:
    71→
    72→For every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a graph $G$ with infinite chromatic number such that
    73→every finite set of $m$ vertices in $G$ contains an independent set of size at least
    74→$(1/2 - \varepsilon) m$.
    75→
    76→This was conjectured by Erdős [Er69b] and follows from a result of Erdős, Hajnal, and
    77→Szemerédi [EHS82].
    78→-/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    80→theorem erdos_750_eps :
    81→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    82→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    83→      (∀ k : ℕ, ¬G.Colorable k) ∧
    84→      ∀ (S : Finset V), ∃ I : Finset V,
    85→        I ⊆ S ∧ G.IsIndepSet ↑I ∧
    86→        (1 / 2 - ε) * (S.card : ℝ) ≤ (I.card : ℝ) := by
    87→  sorry
    88→
    89→/--
    90→Erdős Problem 750 — c > 1/4 variant [ErHa67b]:
    91→
    92→For every $c > 1/4$, there exists a graph $G$ with infinite chromatic number such that
    93→every finite set of $m$ vertices in $G$ contains an independent set of size at least
    94→$(1/2 - c) m$.
    95→
    96→Proved by Erdős and Hajnal [ErHa67b].
    97→-/
    98→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    99→theorem erdos_750_quarter :
   100→    ∀ c : ℝ, c > 1 / 4 →
   101→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
   102→      (∀ k : ℕ, ¬G.Colorable k) ∧
   103→      ∀ (S : Finset V), ∃ I : Finset V,
   104→        I ⊆ S ∧ G.IsIndepSet ↑I ∧
   105→        (1 / 2 - c) * (S.card : ℝ) ≤ (I.card : ℝ) := by
   106→  sorry
   107→
   108→end Erdos750
   109→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 751
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/751](https://www.erdosproblems.com/751)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a graph with chromatic number $\chi(G) = 4$. If $m_1 < m_2 < \cdots$ are the
    25→lengths of the cycles in $G$, can $\min(m_{i+1} - m_i)$ be arbitrarily large? Can this
    26→happen if the girth of $G$ is large?
    27→
    28→The answer is no: Bondy and Vince [BoVi98] proved that every graph with minimum degree
    29→at least $3$ has two cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$, and hence the same is
    30→true for every graph with chromatic number $4$ (regardless of girth).
    31→
    32→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_ (1994).
    33→
    34→[BoVi98] Bondy, J. A. and Vince, A., _Cycles in a graph whose lengths differ by one or
    35→two_. Journal of Graph Theory **27** (1998), 11–15.
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open SimpleGraph
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos751
    41→
    42→/-- The set of cycle lengths occurring in a simple graph. -/
    43→def cycleLengths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    44→  {n | ∃ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = n}
    45→
    46→/-- **Erdős Problem 751**: If $G$ is a graph with $\chi(G) = 4$ and $m_1 < m_2 < \cdots$
    47→are the cycle lengths of $G$, the minimum gap $\min(m_{i+1} - m_i)$ cannot be made
    48→arbitrarily large. Bondy and Vince [BoVi98] proved that every graph with minimum degree
    49→at least $3$ has two cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$, which implies the
    50→negative answer since $\chi(G) = 4$ forces a subgraph with minimum degree $\geq 3$.
    51→
    52→[BoVi98] -/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    54→theorem erdos_751 : answer(False) ↔
    55→    (∀ C : ℕ, ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    56→      G.chromaticNumber = 4 ∧
    57→        ∀ m₁ ∈ cycleLengths G, ∀ m₂ ∈ cycleLengths G,
    58→          m₁ < m₂ → C ≤ m₂ - m₁) := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→/-- **Bondy–Vince Theorem** [BoVi98]: Every graph with minimum degree at least $3$
    62→contains two cycles whose lengths differ by at most $2$. This is the stronger result
    63→that resolves Erdős Problem 751. -/
    64→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_751.variants.bondy_vince {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    66→    [Nonempty V]
    67→    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj]
    68→    (hmin : 3 ≤ G.minDegree) :
    69→    ∃ m₁ ∈ cycleLengths G, ∃ m₂ ∈ cycleLengths G,
    70→      m₁ < m₂ ∧ m₂ - m₁ ≤ 2 := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→/-- **Erdős Problem 751 (girth variant)**: Even if we additionally require the girth of
    74→$G$ to be large, the minimum gap between consecutive cycle lengths in a graph with
    75→$\chi(G) = 4$ cannot be made arbitrarily large. This follows from the Bondy–Vince theorem,
    76→which makes no assumption on girth. -/
    77→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    78→theorem erdos_751.variants.large_girth : answer(False) ↔
    79→    (∀ C : ℕ, ∀ g : ℕ, ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    80→      G.chromaticNumber = 4 ∧ g < G.girth ∧
    81→        ∀ m₁ ∈ cycleLengths G, ∀ m₂ ∈ cycleLengths G,
    82→          m₁ < m₂ → C ≤ m₂ - m₁) := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos751
    86→

     7→

     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 752
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/752](https://www.erdosproblems.com/752)
    23→
    24→A question of Erdős, Faudree, and Schelp. Let $G$ be a graph with minimum degree $k$ and
    25→girth $> 2s$. Must there be $\gg k^s$ many distinct cycle lengths in $G$?
    26→
    27→Erdős, Faudree, and Schelp proved it when $s = 2$.
    28→
    29→Sudakov and Verstraëte proved the full conjecture, and moreover showed that under the weaker
    30→assumption of average degree $k$ and girth $> 2s$, there are $\gg k^s$ many consecutive even
    31→integers that are cycle lengths in $G$.
    32→
    33→Additional thanks to Raphael Steiner.
    34→
    35→[Er92b] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    36→Matematiche (Catania) (1992), 231–240.
    37→
    38→[Er93] Erdős, P., _On some of my favourite theorems_. Combinatorics, Paul Erdős is eighty,
    39→Vol. 2 (Keszthely, 1993), 97–132.
    40→
    41→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    42→Discrete Math. 165/166 (1997), 227–231.
    43→
    44→[SuVe08] Sudakov, B. and Verstraëte, J., _Cycle lengths in sparse graphs_. Combinatorica 28
    45→(2008), 357–372.
    46→-/
    47→
    48→open SimpleGraph
    49→
    50→namespace Erdos752
    51→
    52→/-- The set of cycle lengths occurring in a simple graph. -/
    53→def cycleLengths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Set ℕ :=
    54→  {n | ∃ (v : V) (p : G.Walk v v), p.IsCycle ∧ p.length = n}
    55→
    56→/--
    57→**Erdős–Faudree–Schelp Conjecture (proved by Sudakov–Verstraëte)
    58→(Erdős Problem 752)** [Er92b][Er93][Er94b][SuVe08]:
    59→
    60→There exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that for every finite graph $G$
    61→with minimum degree at least $k$ and girth greater than $2s$, the number of
    62→distinct cycle lengths in $G$ is at least $c \cdot k^s$.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_752 : answer(True) ↔
    66→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    67→    ∀ (V : Type*) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    68→      (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj]
    69→      (k s : ℕ),
    70→      k ≤ G.minDegree →
    71→      2 * s < G.girth →
    72→      c * (k : ℝ) ^ s ≤ ((cycleLengths G).ncard : ℝ) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos752
    76→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 753
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/753](https://www.erdosproblems.com/753)
    23→
    24→Does there exist some constant $c > 0$ such that
    25→$\chi_L(G) + \chi_L(G^c) > n^{1/2+c}$ for every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices?
    26→Disproved by Alon [Al92], who showed that for every $n$, there exists a graph $G$ on
    27→$n$ vertices with $\chi_L(G) + \chi_L(G^c) \leq C \sqrt{n \log n}$ for some absolute
    28→constant $C > 0$.
    29→
    30→[ERT80] Erdős, P., Rubin, A. L., and Taylor, H., _Choosability in graphs_. Proceedings of the
    31→West Coast Conference on Combinatorics, Graph Theory and Computing (1980), 125-157.
    32→
    33→[Al92] Alon, N., _Choice numbers of graphs: a probabilistic approach_. Combinatorics, Probability
    34→and Computing 1 (1992), 107-114.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open SimpleGraph
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos753
    40→
    41→/-- A graph $G$ is $k$-choosable ($k$-list-colorable) if for every assignment of lists
    42→of size at least $k$ to the vertices, there exists a proper coloring where each
    43→vertex receives a color from its list. -/
    44→def IsChoosable {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    45→  ∀ (L : V → Finset ℕ), (∀ v, k ≤ (L v).card) →
    46→    ∃ f : V → ℕ, (∀ v, f v ∈ L v) ∧ (∀ u v, G.Adj u v → f u ≠ f v)
    47→
    48→/-- The list chromatic number (choice number) of a graph: the minimum $k$ such
    49→that $G$ is $k$-choosable. -/
    50→noncomputable def listChromaticNumber {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ :=
    51→  sInf {k : ℕ | IsChoosable G k}
    52→
    53→/--
    54→**Erdős Problem 753** [ERT80]:
    55→
    56→Does there exist some constant $c > 0$ such that
    57→$\chi_L(G) + \chi_L(G^c) > n^{1/2+c}$ for every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices?
    58→Disproved by Alon [Al92].
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    61→theorem erdos_753 :
    62→    answer(False) ↔
    63→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    64→      ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ 2 →
    65→        ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)),
    66→          (listChromaticNumber G + listChromaticNumber Gᶜ : ℝ) >
    67→            (n : ℝ) ^ (1 / 2 + c) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→/--
    71→**Alon's upper bound** [Al92]:
    72→
    73→There exists an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that for every $n \geq 2$, there exists
    74→a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with
    75→$\chi_L(G) + \chi_L(G^c) \leq C \cdot \sqrt{n \cdot \log n}$.
    76→-/
    77→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    78→theorem erdos_753.variants.alon_upper_bound :
    79→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    80→    ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ 2 →
    81→      ∃ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)),
    82→        (listChromaticNumber G + listChromaticNumber Gᶜ : ℝ) ≤
    83→          C * Real.sqrt (n * Real.log n) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos753
    87→

     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 754
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/754](https://www.erdosproblems.com/754)
    23→
    24→Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^4$
    25→in which every $x \in A$ has at least $f(n)$ points in $A$ equidistant from $x$.
    26→
    27→Is it true that $f(n) \leq n/2 + O(1)$?
    28→
    29→Avis, Erdős, and Pach [AEP88] proved that $n/2 + 2 \leq f(n) \leq (1 + o(1))n/2$.
    30→This was proved by Swanepoel [Sw13].
    31→
    32→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems on combinatorial geometry_.
    33→
    34→[AEP88] Avis, D., Erdős, P., and Pach, J., _Repeated distances in space_.
    35→Graphs Combin. (1988), 207–217.
    36→
    37→[Sw13] Swanepoel, K.J., _Favorite distances in high dimensions_. (2013), 499–519.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open Classical
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos754
    43→
    44→/--
    45→**Erdős Problem 754** [Er94b]:
    46→
    47→Let $f(n)$ be the largest $k$ such that there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^4$
    48→where every $x \in A$ has at least $k$ other points in $A$ all at the same distance
    49→from $x$. Is it true that $f(n) \leq n/2 + O(1)$?
    50→
    51→Formulated as: there exists a constant $C$ such that for every finite set
    52→$A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^4$ of $n$ points, if every point $x \in A$ has at least $k$ other
    53→points at a common distance from $x$, then $k \leq n/2 + C$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    56→theorem erdos_754 :
    57→    answer(True) ↔
    58→    ∃ C : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 4)),
    59→      A.card = n →
    60→      ∀ k : ℕ,
    61→        (∀ x ∈ A, ∃ d : ℝ,
    62→          k ≤ (A.filter (fun y => x ≠ y ∧ dist x y = d)).card) →
    63→        (k : ℝ) ≤ n / 2 + C := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→end Erdos754
    67→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 755
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/755](https://www.erdosproblems.com/755)
    23→
    24→The number of equilateral triangles of size $1$ formed by any set of $n$ points
    25→in $\mathbb{R}^6$ is at most $(1/27 + o(1))n^3$.
    26→
    27→A problem of Erdős and Purdy [ErPu75]. Erdős believed the bound should hold
    28→even for equilateral triangles of any size. This was proved in a strong form
    29→by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25b], who showed $T_d(n) = (1/27 + o(1))n^3$
    30→for all even $d \geq 6$.
    31→
    32→See also Problem #1086.
    33→
    34→[ErPu75] Erdős, P. and Purdy, G., _Some extremal problems in geometry. III_.
    35→(1975), 291-308.
    36→
    37→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some problems in number theory, combinatorics and
    38→combinatorial geometry_. Mathematica Pannonica (1994), 261-269.
    39→
    40→[CDL25b] Clemen, F., Dumitrescu, A., and Liu, Y., _The number of regular
    41→simplices in higher dimensions_. arXiv:2507.19841 (2025).
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open Classical
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos755
    47→
    48→/-- The number of unit equilateral triangles determined by a finite point set
    49→in $d$-dimensional Euclidean space: the number of $3$-element subsets where
    50→all pairwise distances are $1$. -/
    51→noncomputable def unitEquilateralTriangleCount {d : ℕ}
    52→    (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))) : ℕ :=
    53→  Set.ncard {T : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)) |
    54→    T ⊆ A ∧ T.card = 3 ∧ ∀ x ∈ T, ∀ y ∈ T, x ≠ y → dist x y = 1}
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 755 [ErPu75][Er94b]:
    58→
    59→The number of unit equilateral triangles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^6$ is
    60→at most $(1/27 + o(1))n^3$.
    61→
    62→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that for all $n \geq N_0$
    63→and every set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^6$, the number of unit equilateral triangles
    64→is at most $(1/27 + \varepsilon) \cdot n^3$.
    65→-/
    66→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    67→theorem erdos_755 :
    68→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    69→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    70→        ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 6)),
    71→          A.card = n →
    72→          (unitEquilateralTriangleCount A : ℝ) ≤ (1 / 27 + ε) * (n : ℝ) ^ 3 := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→/-- The number of equilateral triangles of any size determined by a finite point set
    76→in $d$-dimensional Euclidean space: the number of $3$-element subsets where
    77→all pairwise distances are equal (and positive). -/
    78→noncomputable def equilateralTriangleCount {d : ℕ}
    79→    (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d))) : ℕ :=
    80→  Set.ncard {T : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin d)) |
    81→    T ⊆ A ∧ T.card = 3 ∧ ∃ r > 0, ∀ x ∈ T, ∀ y ∈ T, x ≠ y → dist x y = r}
    82→
    83→/--
    84→Erdős Problem 755, any-size variant [ErPu75][Er94b][CDL25b]:
    85→
    86→The number of equilateral triangles of *any* size determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^6$
    87→is at most $(1/27 + o(1))n^3$.
    88→
    89→This is the stronger version of `erdos_755`, where the equilateral triangles are not
    90→restricted to unit side length. Proved by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25b].
    91→-/
    92→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    93→theorem erdos_755_any_size :
    94→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    95→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    96→        ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 6)),
    97→          A.card = n →
    98→          (equilateralTriangleCount A : ℝ) ≤ (1 / 27 + ε) * (n : ℝ) ^ 3 := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→end Erdos755
   102→

     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 756
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/756](https://www.erdosproblems.com/756)
    23→
    24→Let $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points. Can there be $\gg n$ many
    25→distinct distances each of which occurs for more than $n$ many pairs from $A$?
    26→
    27→[ErPa90] Erdős, P. and Pach, J., _Variations on the theme of repeated distances_,
    28→Combinatorica (1990).
    29→
    30→[Bh24] Bhowmick, K., _A problem of Erdős about rich distances_. arXiv:2407.01174 (2024).
    31→
    32→[CDL25] Clemen, F., Dumitrescu, A., and Liu, D., _On multiplicities of interpoint
    33→distances_. arXiv:2505.04283 (2025).
    34→
    35→See also problems #132 and #957.
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open Classical
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos756
    41→
    42→/-- For a finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ and a real value $d$,
    43→the number of ordered pairs $(x, y)$ with $x \neq y$ in $A$ at
    44→Euclidean distance $d$. -/
    45→noncomputable def pairCount (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) (d : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    46→  ((A ×ˢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ≠ p.2 ∧ dist p.1 p.2 = d)).card
    47→
    48→/-- The set of distances that occur with multiplicity greater than $|A|$
    49→(counting ordered pairs). -/
    50→noncomputable def highMultiplicityDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Set ℝ :=
    51→  {d : ℝ | A.card < pairCount A d}
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Erdős Problem 756 [ErPa90] (proved by Bhowmick [Bh24]):
    55→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
    56→there is a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with the number of
    57→distinct distances occurring more than $n$ times (as ordered pairs) being
    58→at least $c \cdot n$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    61→theorem erdos_756 : answer(True) ↔
    62→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    63→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    64→      ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), A.card = n ∧
    65→        c * (n : ℝ) ≤ Set.ncard (highMultiplicityDistances A) := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Stronger variant of Erdős Problem 756 (Erdős–Pach [ErPa90]): for sufficiently
    70→large $n$, there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that every
    71→realized distance except the largest occurs with multiplicity greater than $|A|$
    72→(as ordered pairs). Hopf and Pannwitz (1934) showed the largest distance occurs
    73→at most $n$ times, so it must be excluded.
    74→-/
    75→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    76→theorem erdos_756_all_except_largest :
    77→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    78→      ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), A.card = n ∧
    79→        ∀ d : ℝ, 0 < pairCount A d →
    80→          (∃ x ∈ A, ∃ y ∈ A, x ≠ y ∧ dist x y > d) →
    81→          A.card < pairCount A d := by
    82→  sorry
    83→
    84→end Erdos756
    85→
